The following collection of questions concerns the design of a randomized experiment where the
units to be randomized to drug A or drug B are people, for whom we have a large number of background covariates, collectively labelled

(e.g., age, sex, blood pressure, height, weight, occupational status, history of heart disease, family history of heart disease). The objective is to assign approximately half to drug A and half to drug B where the means of each of the

variables (and means of non-linear functions of them, such as squares or products) are close to equal in the two groups. Instead of using classical methods of design, such as blocking or stratification, the plan is to use modern computers to try many random allocations and discard those allocations that are considered unacceptable according to a pre-determined criterion for balanced
means, in particular an affinely invariant measure such as the Mahalanobis distance between the means of
in the two groups. After an acceptable allocation is found, outcome variables will be measured, and their means will be compared in group A and group B to estimate a treatment effect.
